_metadata.description: Deterministic ingredient-level interaction rules keyed by canonical database identity
_metadata.structure_note: One entry per ingredient (or per form-scoped variant). All conditions, drug classes, and dose thresholds for that ingredient are in one object.

condition_rules.mechanism: 7-Keto DHEA has hormonal activity concerns and is not established as safe during pregnancy.
condition_rules.action: Do not use during pregnancy unless directed by an obstetric specialist.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Hormonal activity and limited lactation safety data support avoiding use.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid during lactation due to insufficient safety evidence and endocrine-active profile.

condition_rules.mechanism: Ephedra has serious cardiovascular and neurologic risks and is FDA-banned as a supplement ingredient.
condition_rules.action: Do not use in pregnancy.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Adrenergic stimulation can antagonize blood-pressure control therapies.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid use with antihypertensives.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Serious toxicity risk profile and regulatory prohibition for supplement use.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Ephedra-containing supplements should not be used.

condition_rules.mechanism: Synephrine is structurally similar to ephedrine, acts on adrenergic receptors, and significantly increases systolic and diastolic blood pressure, especially when combined with caffeine.
condition_rules.action: Avoid bitter orange / synephrine supplements. Commonly found in weight-loss and pre-workout products as an ephedra substitute.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Sympathomimetic effects directly counteract blood pressure-lowering therapy.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid combination with antihypertensive medications.

condition_rules.mechanism: Adrenergic stimulation can raise blood pressure and heart rate.
condition_rules.action: Do not use in hypertension or cardiovascular risk settings.

condition_rules.mechanism: Stimulant profile and hemodynamic effects present maternal-fetal risk concerns.
condition_rules.action: Do not use during pregnancy.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Yohimbe can counter blood pressure-lowering therapy.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid combination with antihypertensive medications.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Safety data are limited and stimulant effects are concerning.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid in pregnancy and lactation.

condition_rules.mechanism: Propylene glycol is metabolized to lactic acid via alcohol dehydrogenase and ~40-50% is renally excreted. Renal impairment reduces clearance, leading to accumulation and lactic acidosis.
condition_rules.action: Prefer products without propylene glycol when chronic kidney disease is present.

condition_rules.mechanism: Oral aloe (gel/latex/whole leaf extract) has safety concerns and may be unsafe in pregnancy.
condition_rules.action: Avoid oral aloe supplements during pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Aloe gel polysaccharides (acemannan) may stimulate insulin secretion and improve glucose uptake. Limited clinical evidence but preclinical data is suggestive.
condition_rules.action: Monitor glucose if combining oral aloe supplements with diabetes medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Possible additive glucose-lowering. Oral aloe may also alter drug absorption via laxative effects.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor glucose and GI symptoms.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: NCCIH indicates oral aloe may be unsafe in pregnancy and breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: This rule is intended for oral/systemic supplement use, not topical-only products.

condition_rules.mechanism: Enhances insulin-stimulated glucose uptake via GLUT4 translocation and reduces oxidative stress-mediated insulin resistance. May potentiate glucose-lowering effects of diabetes medications.
condition_rules.action: Monitor blood glucose when initiating ALA at doses >= 600 mg/day alongside diabetes medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive insulin-sensitizing effect may increase hypoglycemia risk, especially with insulin and sulfonylureas.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor glucose and adjust medication only under clinician supervision.

condition_rules.mechanism: Berberine activates AMPK, increases insulin sensitivity, and stimulates GLP-1 secretion. Produces glucose-lowering effects comparable to metformin. Additive hypoglycemia risk is clinically significant.
condition_rules.action: Do not combine with diabetes medications without physician oversight. Monitor blood glucose closely.

condition_rules.mechanism: Berberine activates PCSK9 degradation, upregulates LDL receptors, and has demonstrated significant lipid-lowering effects in multiple RCTs, including reductions in total cholesterol, LDL-C, and triglycerides comparable to some statins. This makes berberine a candidate adjunct for hypercholesterolemia management.
condition_rules.action: Berberine may lower LDL cholesterol meaningfully. If combined with statins, monitor lipid panel and be alert to additive effects. Discuss with prescriber.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive glucose-lowering comparable to metformin. Also inhibits CYP3A4/CYP2D6, potentially increasing drug levels of co-administered medications.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid unsupervised co-administration. Requires clinician-guided glucose monitoring and potential dose adjustment.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Berberine inhibits CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein, which are primary metabolic and transport pathways for many statins (simvastatin, atorvastatin, lovastatin). Inhibition of CYP3A4 can increase plasma statin concentrations, potentially raising myopathy and rhabdomyolysis risk. Additive LDL-lowering also occurs.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid combining berberine with high-dose CYP3A4-metabolized statins without prescriber oversight. If combined, start with lower statin doses and monitor for muscle symptoms (myalgia, weakness, dark urine).

drug_class_rules._note: This is an ADDITION to the existing RULE_IQM_BERBERINE_DIABETES rule. Add this object to the drug_class_rules array of that rule.

drug_class_rules_addition.mechanism: Berberine inhibits MAO-A and MAO-B activity in vitro, producing additive monoamine oxidase inhibition when combined with MAOI antidepressants (phenelzine, tranylcypromine, selegiline). This combined MAO inhibition can elevate serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine to toxic levels, increasing risk of serotonin syndrome (agitation, hyperthermia, clonus, autonomic instability) and hypertensive crisis. Additionally, berberine inhibits CYP2D6 and CYP3A4, reducing metabolic clearance of MAOIs.
drug_class_rules_addition.action: Avoid combining berberine with MAOIs. If transitioning from MAOI to berberine-containing supplements, observe a minimum 14-day washout. Alert prescriber to use.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Berberine (as in goldenseal) has documented neonatal bilirubin/kernicterus risk concerns.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid berberine-containing supplements during pregnancy and lactation.

condition_rules.mechanism: Contains charantin, polypeptide-p (plant insulin analog), and vicine which stimulate beta cells and increase glucose utilization. RCT showed significant fasting glucose reduction.
condition_rules.action: Additive hypoglycemia risk with diabetes medications. Do not use as a substitute for prescribed medications. Monitor glucose.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive glucose-lowering effect, especially with sulfonylureas and insulin.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for hypoglycemia and coordinate with prescriber.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Animal studies suggest abortifacient and embryotoxic effects.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid during pregnancy and lactation.

condition_rules.mechanism: Black cohosh may have uterotonic/hormonal effects and is not considered safe in pregnancy.
condition_rules.action: Avoid use during pregnancy unless explicitly directed by obstetric care team.

condition_rules.mechanism: Cimicifuga racemosa (black cohosh) causes idiosyncratic hepatitis via two overlapping mechanisms: (1) oxidative stress generating 4-hydroxynonenal (4HNE) protein adducts in hepatocytes that act as autoantigens, triggering autoimmune troxis necrosis by T-lymphocytes; and (2) mitochondrial toxicity impairing beta-oxidation and membrane potential at clinical concentrations. In patients with pre-existing liver disease, these injury pathways are amplified by baseline hepatic dysfunction and reduced regenerative capacity, increasing risk of severe or fulminant outcomes.
condition_rules.action: Avoid black cohosh in patients with liver disease of any etiology. If a patient develops unexplained liver enzyme elevation while taking black cohosh, discontinue immediately and evaluate for DILI.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Pregnancy safety is uncertain and potential adverse effects are described in safety guidance.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid in pregnancy; lactation evidence is limited.

condition_rules.mechanism: Caffeine crosses the placenta and higher daily intake is associated with pregnancy risk concerns.
condition_rules.action: Keep supplemental caffeine low and account for all dietary caffeine sources.

condition_rules.mechanism: Caffeine causes acute blood pressure elevation (3-15 mmHg systolic) through adenosine receptor antagonism and sympathetic stimulation.
condition_rules.action: Limit caffeine-containing supplements. Monitor BP when starting caffeine supplements.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: High caffeine exposure can affect maternal-fetal and infant outcomes.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: This threshold applies to supplemental caffeine only; total daily caffeine from beverages/foods should be considered.

condition_rules.mechanism: Chromium can alter glucose metabolism and insulin sensitivity.
condition_rules.action: Use with glucose monitoring if on diabetes treatment.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Potential additive glucose-lowering effect.
drug_class_rules.action: Adjust medication only under clinician supervision.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Chromium picolinate can reduce levothyroxine absorption when taken concurrently. Divalent chromium binds to thyroid hormone molecules in the gastrointestinal tract.
drug_class_rules.action: Separate chromium supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.

condition_rules.mechanism: Cinnamon may lower blood glucose in some individuals.
condition_rules.action: Track glucose trends when adding supplemental cinnamon.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Potential additive glucose-lowering effect with diabetes medications.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for hypoglycemia and coordinate with prescriber.

condition_rules.mechanism: Dong quai is traditionally associated with uterine and hormone-active effects; pregnancy safety is not established.
condition_rules.action: Avoid during pregnancy unless specifically directed by obstetric clinician.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Dong quai contains coumarin derivatives (osthole, ferulic acid) that inhibit platelet aggregation. Case report documented >2-fold INR elevation in warfarin-stabilized patient after 4 weeks of dong quai.
drug_class_rules.action: Use caution with anticoagulant therapy. Monitor INR closely.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Clinical pregnancy/lactation safety data are limited and precautionary avoidance is recommended.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Use only with clinician oversight during pregnancy or lactation.

condition_rules.mechanism: Galactomannan fiber delays carbohydrate absorption and 4-hydroxyisoleucine stimulates insulin secretion. Meta-analysis shows FBG reduction of 0.96 mmol/L.
condition_rules.action: Additive blood sugar lowering with diabetes medications. Separate dosing from oral medications by 2 hours (fiber impairs drug absorption). Monitor glucose.

condition_rules.mechanism: Fenugreek is one of the most widely used galactagogues globally. Proposed mechanism involves stimulation of sweat glands (structurally similar to mammary glands) via phytoestrogens, and possibly dopaminergic pathways affecting prolactin. While some women report increased milk volume, a 2022 Australian cross-sectional survey found widespread use with incomplete evidence of efficacy. Fenugreek's volatile compounds (diosgenin, 4-hydroxyisoleucine) are excreted in breast milk and may cause GI upset (colic, diarrhea, unusual maple-syrup body odor) in the nursing infant.
condition_rules.action: Fenugreek is widely used as a galactagogue but evidence for efficacy is inconsistent. If used, monitor nursing infant for GI symptoms (excessive gas, diarrhea, colic). Do not use in infants with maple syrup urine disease family history. Avoid doses >3g/day without lactation consultant guidance.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive glucose-lowering and fiber-mediated delay of oral medication absorption.
drug_class_rules.action: Separate dosing from oral hypoglycemics by 2 hours. Monitor for hypoglycemia.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Traditional concern for uterotonic effects. May also lower blood sugar in pregnant women on glucose management.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid during pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Tanacetum parthenium parthenolide inhibits platelet aggregation and has demonstrated uterotonic activity. Historical emmenagogue use is well-documented. Cochrane and ACOG recommend avoidance during pregnancy.
condition_rules.action: Do not use during pregnancy.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Parthenolide inhibits platelet aggregation via thromboxane synthase; additive bleeding risk with anticoagulant/antiplatelet therapy.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for increased bleeding tendency.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Parthenolide crosses biological membranes and has uterotonic and anti-platelet effects. No safety data in lactation.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Feverfew is contraindicated in pregnancy per NCCIH and traditional classification as emmenagogue. Avoid during lactation due to insufficient safety data.

condition_rules.mechanism: Viscous soluble fiber delays gastric emptying and slows glucose absorption. Meta-analysis of 6 RCTs showed significant reductions in FBG (-1.08 mmol/L) and 2h postprandial glucose.
condition_rules.action: Separate from oral diabetes medications by at least 2 hours (fiber delays drug absorption). Take with adequate water.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Fiber delays absorption of co-administered oral medications including metformin and sulfonylureas.
drug_class_rules.action: Separate glucomannan from oral diabetes medications by at least 2 hours.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: High dietary fiber intake adsorbs levothyroxine in the gut, reducing absorption. Psyllium and other supplemental fibers taken simultaneously with thyroid medication can significantly impair bioavailability.
drug_class_rules.action: Separate fiber supplements from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.

condition_rules.mechanism: Garlic can reduce platelet aggregation and increase bleeding tendency around procedures.
condition_rules.action: Stop before elective surgery unless clinician directs otherwise.

condition_rules.mechanism: Garlic lowers blood pressure via hydrogen sulfide-mediated vasodilation and ACE inhibition (8-10 mmHg systolic). Additive effect with antihypertensives may cause symptomatic hypotension.
condition_rules.action: Inform prescriber if taking garlic supplements alongside BP medications. Monitor for dizziness or orthostatic hypotension.

condition_rules.mechanism: Allicin and other garlic organosulfur compounds inhibit HMG-CoA reductase and cholesterol synthesis. Meta-analyses of RCTs demonstrate modest but significant reductions in total cholesterol (approximately 17 mg/dL) and LDL-C. The effect is moderate and not sufficient as monotherapy for significant hypercholesterolemia.
condition_rules.action: Garlic supplements may contribute modest cholesterol-lowering. If used alongside lipid-lowering medication, monitor lipid panel. Not a substitute for prescribed therapy.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Potential additive anticoagulant/antiplatelet effects.
drug_class_rules.action: Use only with clinician monitoring for bleeding signs.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Potential additive platelet inhibition.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for bruising or bleeding.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive blood pressure lowering may cause symptomatic hypotension.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor BP when combining garlic supplements with antihypertensive medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Garlic may weakly inhibit CYP3A4 and CYP2C9, potentially increasing plasma levels of some statins metabolized by these enzymes. Additionally, additive LDL-lowering effects may occur. The clinical magnitude of the pharmacokinetic interaction is considered modest but relevant at high garlic extract doses.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor lipid levels when combining garlic supplements with statin therapy. Report any muscle symptoms.

condition_rules.mechanism: Ginger may influence platelet function at supplemental doses.
condition_rules.action: Discuss use before surgery.

condition_rules.mechanism: Ginger inhibits thromboxane A2 (TXA2) synthesis and PAF-induced platelet aggregation. In patients with inherited or acquired bleeding disorders, this antiplatelet activity increases hemorrhage risk.
condition_rules.action: Use ginger supplements with caution in bleeding disorders. Monitor for bruising or prolonged bleeding.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Potential additive bleeding risk with anticoagulants.
drug_class_rules.action: Use with monitoring and clinician input.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive antiplatelet effect: ginger TXA2 inhibition combined with aspirin COX-1 or clopidogrel ADP pathway inhibition.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for increased bleeding risk when combining ginger with antiplatelet drugs.

condition_rules.mechanism: Ginkgo biloba increases hepatic CYP450 activity, accelerating metabolism of calcium channel blockers (nicardipine studied) and reducing their antihypertensive effect.
condition_rules.action: Inform prescriber. Blood pressure may be less well-controlled. Dose adjustment may be needed.

condition_rules.mechanism: Ginkgo biloba seeds and insufficiently processed leaf products contain ginkgotoxin (4-O-methylpyridoxine), which inhibits GABA synthesis by depleting pyridoxal-5-phosphate cofactor, producing proconvulsant effects. Standardized 24% flavonoid / 6% terpene leaf extracts with ginkgotoxin removed are lower risk, but purity cannot be guaranteed from all manufacturers.
condition_rules.action: Use ginkgo with caution or avoid in seizure disorders. Use only standardized ginkgo leaf extract from reputable manufacturers. Monitor for increased seizure frequency.

condition_rules.mechanism: Ginkgolides (especially ginkgolide B) inhibit platelet-activating factor (PAF), reducing platelet aggregation and extending bleeding time. Multiple case reports of spontaneous hemorrhage including subdural hematoma.
condition_rules.action: Avoid ginkgo with bleeding disorders. If used, monitor for bruising, prolonged bleeding, or unexplained headache.

condition_rules.mechanism: PAF inhibition by ginkgolides increases perioperative bleeding risk. Discontinue at least 2 weeks before scheduled surgery.
condition_rules.action: Discontinue ginkgo at least 14 days before surgery.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Increases metabolism of calcium channel blockers, reducing their plasma levels and antihypertensive effect.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor BP control when combining with calcium channel blockers.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Ginkgolide B inhibits PAF; co-administration with warfarin or DOACs increases hemorrhage risk. Case reports of subdural hematoma with ginkgo + warfarin.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor INR closely if ginkgo is used with anticoagulants. Consider avoiding combination.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive antiplatelet effect via PAF inhibition combined with COX/ADP pathway inhibition from aspirin/clopidogrel.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for increased bruising or bleeding. Inform prescriber.

condition_rules.mechanism: NCCIH reports some evidence of pregnancy safety concerns and expert caution for use.
condition_rules.action: Avoid routine use during pregnancy unless clinician-directed.

condition_rules.mechanism: Ginsenosides improve insulin sensitivity and increase pancreatic beta-cell insulin secretion. RCTs show significant postprandial glucose reduction.
condition_rules.action: Monitor glucose levels when combining with diabetes medications.

condition_rules.mechanism: Ginseng may affect platelet aggregation and coagulation parameters. Case reports of reduced warfarin INR documented.
condition_rules.action: Stop ginseng at least 7 days before elective surgery. Inform surgeon.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Mild additive glucose-lowering effect.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for hypoglycemia symptoms.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Case reports documented reduced warfarin INR with concurrent ginseng use, possibly via CYP enzyme induction.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor INR closely if combining with warfarin. Inform prescriber.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Pregnancy/lactation safety data are limited and concerns are noted in safety reviews.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Prefer avoidance unless obstetric team approves.

condition_rules.mechanism: Goldenseal (berberine-containing) safety concern includes newborn bilirubin/kernicterus risk and should not be used in pregnancy.
condition_rules.action: Avoid goldenseal during pregnancy.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: NCCIH safety summary advises against use in pregnancy and breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid use in pregnancy and breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: Green tea catechins (EGCG) inhibit intestinal OATP transporters, reducing oral bioavailability of nadolol and potentially other beta-blockers, diminishing BP control.
condition_rules.action: Avoid taking green tea extract supplements close in time to beta-blockers. Separate doses by several hours.

condition_rules.mechanism: EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate), the principal catechin in green tea extract, undergoes hepatic first-pass metabolism. At supplemental doses (≥800 mg EGCG/day), EGCG saturates first-pass elimination and reaches hepatotoxic concentrations, inducing mitochondrial oxidative stress, hepatocellular pattern of liver injury, and transaminase elevations. The United States Pharmacopeia 2020 comprehensive review confirmed dose-dependent hepatocellular toxicity with adverse events reported from 140 mg to ~1,000 mg EGCG/day. Pre-existing liver disease impairs EGCG clearance and lowers the threshold for injury.
condition_rules.action: Avoid concentrated green tea extract supplements (standardized EGCG ≥400 mg/dose) in patients with liver disease. Brewed green tea is not implicated. Recommend liver function monitoring if use cannot be avoided.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: EGCG inhibits OATP transporters, reducing beta-blocker absorption and potentially compromising BP control.
drug_class_rules.action: Separate green tea extract from beta-blocker dosing by at least 2-3 hours.

condition_rules.mechanism: Gymnemic acids reduce intestinal glucose absorption and may stimulate beta-cell regeneration and insulin secretion. Clinical studies show FBG and HbA1c reductions.
condition_rules.action: Additive hypoglycemia risk with oral hypoglycemics and insulin. Dose adjustments may be necessary. Monitor glucose.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive glucose-lowering effect via multiple mechanisms.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor blood glucose closely when combining with diabetes medications.

condition_rules.mechanism: Hawthorn extract has mild antihypertensive properties via endothelium-dependent vasodilation and mild ACE-inhibitory activity.
condition_rules.action: Inform prescriber. Additive BP lowering may occur with antihypertensives.

condition_rules.mechanism: Hawthorn (Crataegus) has positive inotropic and mild chronotropic effects via phosphodiesterase inhibition and increased calcium sensitivity. It is used as an adjunct in mild heart failure (NYHA Class I-II). The SPICE trial (post-LVEF <35%) did not show mortality benefit. Hawthorn may potentiate or interact with cardiac glycosides via additive effects on cardiac contractility and the potential for P-glycoprotein interactions affecting digoxin pharmacokinetics.
condition_rules.action: Hawthorn may be used as an adjunct in mild heart failure under physician supervision. Inform cardiologist if taking alongside digoxin or other cardiac medications. Monitor for bradycardia or hypotension.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive blood pressure lowering from mild ACE-inhibitory and vasodilatory activity.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor BP regularly during concurrent use.

condition_rules.mechanism: Iodine is essential in pregnancy, but excessive intake can adversely affect maternal/fetal thyroid function.
condition_rules.action: Keep total iodine intake within guideline ranges; avoid excess supplementation.

condition_rules.mechanism: Excess iodine triggers the Wolff-Chaikoff effect, acutely suppressing thyroid hormone synthesis. Individuals with pre-existing thyroid disease (Hashimoto thyroiditis, Graves disease) are particularly susceptible to iodine-induced hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism. Kelp/seaweed products contain highly variable iodine loads (50–500% of stated dose) and carry additional risk.
condition_rules.action: Avoid high-dose iodine or kelp/seaweed supplements in thyroid disorders without physician oversight. Routine supplementation above the RDA (150 mcg/day) is not recommended.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: High iodine intake can alter thyroid physiology and therapy response.
drug_class_rules.action: Coordinate iodine supplements with thyroid medication management.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Both deficiency and excess can be harmful; UL and recommended intake should guide use.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Threshold escalates only on excess; baseline reflects required-but-dose-sensitive nutrient status.

condition_rules.mechanism: The liver is the principal organ of iron storage and regulation. In liver disease, hepatic hepcidin production is impaired, disrupting iron homeostasis and increasing intestinal iron absorption. Iron supplementation in this context risks exacerbating hepatic iron overload, which drives oxidative stress via the Fenton reaction (Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ → Fe³⁺ + OH⁻ + •OH), lipid peroxidation, and progressive liver fibrosis. This mechanism is most critical in hereditary hemochromatosis and alcoholic liver disease, where iron accumulation is already elevated, but applies broadly to chronic liver disease including NAFLD/NASH, hepatitis C, and cirrhosis.
condition_rules.action: Avoid routine iron supplementation in liver disease patients unless iron deficiency anemia is confirmed by labs (serum iron, ferritin, TIBC). Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration. Monitor ferritin and transferrin saturation regularly.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Iron chelates with methyldopa and certain ACE inhibitors (captopril) in the GI tract, reducing drug absorption and bioavailability.
drug_class_rules.action: Separate iron supplement from methyldopa or captopril by at least 2 hours.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Ferrous sulfate and other iron salts form stable insoluble complexes with levothyroxine, reducing thyroid hormone absorption. Concurrent ingestion can significantly impair replacement therapy efficacy.
drug_class_rules.action: Separate iron supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.

condition_rules.mechanism: L-arginine increases nitric oxide production, causing vasodilation and BP reduction (5-7 mmHg systolic). Additive effect with antihypertensives risks excessive hypotension.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution alongside antihypertensives. Start low and monitor BP.

condition_rules.mechanism: L-arginine is the substrate for nitric oxide synthase (NOS), producing NO which causes vasodilation. While this may benefit endothelial function in heart failure or coronary artery disease, the VINTAGE MI trial (post-MI patients) found unexpected excess deaths in the L-arginine group (6 vs 0 in placebo), leading to early termination. The mechanism may involve arrhythmogenic properties of NO at high concentrations in the post-infarction myocardium.
condition_rules.action: Avoid L-arginine supplementation in the immediate post-myocardial infarction period. Use with caution and only under cardiologist supervision in other heart disease settings.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive vasodilatory effect may enhance hypotensive action beyond intended levels.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for dizziness or lightheadedness. Discuss with prescriber.

condition_rules.mechanism: Glycyrrhizin can increase sodium retention and blood pressure. NOTE: DGL (deglycyrrhizinated licorice) has glycyrrhizin removed and does NOT have this interaction. This rule applies only to whole/standard licorice extract containing glycyrrhizin.
condition_rules.action: Avoid in uncontrolled blood pressure unless deglycyrrhizinated form is confirmed.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: May blunt blood pressure control from antihypertensive therapy. Does not apply to DGL (deglycyrrhizinated) licorice.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor blood pressure and review supplement use with clinician.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Glycyrrhizin inhibits placental 11-beta-HSD2, allowing excess maternal cortisol to reach the fetus. Finnish cohort studies documented >2-fold preterm delivery risk and neurodevelopmental harm (IQ reduction, ADHD risk) with heavy glycyrrhizin exposure.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid during pregnancy. Even confectionery licorice at sustained intake poses risk.

condition_rules.mechanism: Magnesium acts as a natural calcium channel blocker, producing modest BP reductions (2-4 mmHg systolic). Additive effect with pharmaceutical calcium channel blockers may enhance hypotensive action.
condition_rules.action: Generally safe at recommended doses. Inform prescriber if supplementing alongside calcium channel blockers. Monitor for dizziness.

condition_rules.mechanism: Magnesium plays a critical role in cardiac electrophysiology, stabilizing cardiac membranes and modulating ion channels. Hypomagnesemia is associated with cardiac arrhythmias. However, in patients with heart disease who also have renal impairment (common comorbidity), magnesium supplements can accumulate and cause hypermagnesemia, leading to bradycardia, cardiac conduction block, and potentially cardiac arrest.
condition_rules.action: Use magnesium supplements with caution in heart disease patients, especially those with reduced kidney function. Do not exceed recommended doses without renal function assessment. Monitor for signs of hypermagnesemia (weakness, bradycardia).

condition_rules.mechanism: Impaired renal magnesium excretion in CKD (especially eGFR <30) leads to hypermagnesemia accumulation. Supplemental magnesium doses safe in healthy adults can cause life-threatening hypermagnesemia in CKD patients (cardiac arrest, respiratory failure).
condition_rules.action: Avoid magnesium supplementation in severe CKD (eGFR <30) without nephrologist guidance. In mild-moderate CKD, use with caution and monitor serum Mg levels.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive blood pressure lowering from natural calcium channel blocking activity.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor BP when supplementing alongside antihypertensives.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Divalent magnesium cations can bind levothyroxine and impair gastrointestinal absorption, similar to other divalent mineral-levothyroxine chelation interactions.
drug_class_rules.action: Separate magnesium supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.

condition_rules.mechanism: Potassium supplements combined with ACE inhibitors, ARBs, or potassium-sparing diuretics can cause life-threatening hyperkalemia.
condition_rules.action: Do not take potassium supplements without physician approval if on ACE inhibitors, ARBs, or potassium-sparing diuretics.

condition_rules.mechanism: Impaired renal potassium excretion greatly increases hyperkalemia risk with supplemental potassium.
condition_rules.action: Avoid potassium supplements with kidney disease unless directed by nephrologist.

condition_rules.mechanism: Heart failure patients are frequently treated with ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and/or aldosterone antagonists (spironolactone, eplerenone), all of which reduce renal potassium excretion. Adding supplemental potassium to these regimens substantially increases the risk of life-threatening hyperkalemia (>6.0 mEq/L), which can cause ventricular arrhythmias and cardiac arrest.
condition_rules.action: Do not take potassium supplements without physician approval and serum potassium monitoring if on ACE inhibitors, ARBs, or potassium-sparing diuretics. This is especially critical in heart failure management.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: ACE inhibitors and ARBs reduce aldosterone, decreasing renal potassium excretion. Adding exogenous potassium raises serum K+ to dangerous levels.
drug_class_rules.action: Requires serum potassium monitoring. Do not supplement without clinician supervision.

condition_rules.mechanism: Soluble viscous fiber delays gastric emptying and slows carbohydrate absorption, reducing postprandial glucose spikes. RCT showed 11% reduction in all-day postprandial glucose.
condition_rules.action: Separate dosing from oral diabetes medications by at least 2 hours (fiber delays drug absorption). Monitor glucose.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Fiber delays absorption of co-administered oral medications including metformin and sulfonylureas.
drug_class_rules.action: Separate psyllium from oral diabetes medications by at least 2 hours.

condition_rules.mechanism: NCCIH notes potential increased birth-defect risk and major drug interaction potential.
condition_rules.action: Avoid St. John’s wort during pregnancy unless specialist-supervised.

condition_rules.mechanism: St. John's Wort induces CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein, reducing plasma levels of calcium channel blockers (nifedipine, amlodipine, verapamil) and potentially other antihypertensives.
condition_rules.action: Avoid if taking calcium channel blockers or other CYP3A4-metabolized BP medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Potent CYP3A4/P-gp induction reduces plasma levels of calcium channel blockers and other CYP3A4-metabolized antihypertensives, compromising BP control.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid combination. If SJW is discontinued, BP medications may suddenly become more potent, risking hypotension.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: CYP3A4/P-gp induction reduces warfarin plasma levels, decreasing anticoagulant effect. Documented INR reductions requiring warfarin dose increases.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid combination with warfarin and other anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: CYP3A4 induction dramatically reduces cyclosporine, tacrolimus, and other immunosuppressant levels. Documented transplant rejections attributed to SJW co-administration.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not use with immunosuppressants. Risk of organ transplant rejection.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: CYP3A4 induction reduces plasma levels of simvastatin, atorvastatin, and other CYP3A4-metabolized statins.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid with CYP3A4-metabolized statins or monitor lipid levels closely.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: CYP3A4 induction reduces levels of benzodiazepines (alprazolam, midazolam). Also risk of serotonin syndrome if combined with SSRIs/SNRIs.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid unsupervised combination with sedatives or antidepressants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: St John's Wort increases serotonin levels via monoamine reuptake inhibition. Combined with SSRIs/SNRIs, this causes additive serotonergic stimulation leading to serotonin syndrome — a potentially fatal toxidrome (hyperthermia, clonus, agitation, seizures). Multiple case reports and pharmacovigilance alerts.
drug_class_rules.action: Do NOT combine St John's Wort with any SSRI or SNRI. If switching, allow 2-week washout. This is a life-threatening interaction.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: St John's Wort has weak MAOI activity. Combined with pharmaceutical MAOIs, risk of serotonin syndrome and hypertensive crisis.
drug_class_rules.action: Do NOT combine St John's Wort with MAOIs. Life-threatening interaction.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Potential fetal risk and broad CYP/P-gp interactions.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Use in pregnancy should be avoided outside specialist care.

condition_rules.mechanism: Vanadium is insulin-mimetic: activates insulin receptor substrate, inhibits PTP-1B, and reduces hepatic glucose production. Clinical studies showed FBG and HbA1c reductions at 150 mg/day.
condition_rules.action: Additive hypoglycemia risk with insulin and oral hypoglycemics. GI side effects common. Use only under medical supervision.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Insulin-mimetic activity creates additive hypoglycemia risk. Narrow therapeutic window.
drug_class_rules.action: Use only under clinician supervision with glucose monitoring.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Limited safety data and insulin-mimetic activity present risk concerns.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid during pregnancy and lactation.

form_scope: vitamin A from cod liver oil

condition_rules.mechanism: Excess preformed vitamin A intake in pregnancy is associated with teratogenic risk.
condition_rules.action: Avoid high-dose preformed vitamin A in pregnancy unless clinician-directed.

condition_rules.mechanism: Retinol (preformed vitamin A) is stored in hepatic stellate cells (Ito cells) as retinyl esters. Chronic liver disease impairs retinol clearance and stellate cell function, causing accumulation even at doses below standard UL. Hypervitaminosis A induces stellate cell hypertrophy and proliferation, leading to progressive fibrosis, non-cirrhotic portal hypertension, and eventual cirrhosis. CYP2E1 induction (common in alcoholic liver disease) enhances retinol hepatotoxicity via microsomal oxidative activation. Clinically relevant injury has been reported at supplemental doses ≥25,000 IU/day in individuals with underlying liver disease.
condition_rules.action: Limit supplemental preformed vitamin A (retinol/retinyl esters) to ≤5,000 IU/day in patients with liver disease. Beta-carotene is preferred. Monitor LFTs and clinical signs of hypervitaminosis (hepatomegaly, ascites). Avoid multivitamins with high retinol doses.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Dose and form dependent risk is primarily tied to preformed retinoids.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Applies to preformed vitamin A forms; carotenoids are not handled by this threshold rule.

condition_rules.mechanism: At pharmacologic doses (>=1g/day), niacin increases hepatic glucose output and decreases insulin sensitivity. FPG increases of 4-5% and HbA1c increases up to 0.3% documented.
condition_rules.action: Can worsen glycemic control. Monitor glucose after initiating niacin or increasing dose. Low-dose niacin in multivitamins is not a concern.

condition_rules.mechanism: Niacin at doses exceeding 2 g/day causes dose-dependent hepatotoxicity via impairment of mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation and calcium homeostasis disruption. Sustained-release formulations are associated with higher hepatotoxicity risk than immediate-release at equivalent doses. Case reports include acute fulminant hepatic failure requiring ICU admission. In patients with pre-existing liver disease, baseline impaired hepatic metabolism reduces the threshold for injury, and niacin-induced vasodilatation may also exacerbate portal hypertension.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in liver disease; doses above 500 mg/day should require physician supervision and baseline LFTs. Avoid sustained-release niacin formulations in patients with liver disease. Discontinue if ALT/AST elevations exceed 3x ULN.

condition_rules.mechanism: Pharmacologic-dose niacin (≥1g/day) raises HDL-C and lowers triglycerides and LDL-C, but large RCTs (AIM-HIGH, HPS2-THRIVE) failed to show cardiovascular benefit when added to statin therapy, and HPS2-THRIVE found excess serious adverse events with niacin. Extended-release niacin combined with laropiprant was withdrawn in Europe. At doses ≥1g/day, niacin also impairs glucose tolerance.
condition_rules.action: High-dose niacin for cholesterol management has fallen out of favor due to lack of incremental cardiovascular benefit on top of statin therapy and significant side effects. Consult prescriber before initiating pharmacologic niacin for lipid management.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Niacin can raise blood glucose, potentially requiring adjustment of diabetes medication doses.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor glucose closely. Diabetes medication dose adjustment may be needed.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: High-dose niacin combined with statins increases the risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis beyond that of statin monotherapy. This risk is particularly elevated with gemfibrozil-statin combinations, but niacin-statin combinations also carry increased myopathy risk, estimated at approximately 0.12% incidence in combination therapy trials. The mechanism involves pharmacodynamic additive muscle toxicity.
drug_class_rules.action: If high-dose niacin (≥1g/day) is co-prescribed with statins, monitor for myopathy symptoms (muscle pain, weakness, dark urine). Obtain baseline and periodic CK levels. Avoid high-intensity statin doses in this combination.

condition_rules.mechanism: Vitamin C is essential, but excessive supplemental intake above UL is not recommended in pregnancy.
condition_rules.action: Keep vitamin C intake within guideline ranges; avoid high-dose supplementation without clinical need.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Dose-dependent tolerability; excessive daily intake can increase adverse-effect risk.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Threshold escalates only when intake exceeds adult UL.

condition_rules.mechanism: Vitamin E is essential, but excessive supplemental intake above established UL is not recommended in pregnancy.
condition_rules.action: Keep total daily vitamin E intake within guideline ranges and avoid megadosing.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Dose-dependent risk profile; high intake can increase adverse-effect risk.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Threshold escalates only when intake exceeds adult UL.

condition_rules.mechanism: Vitamin K intake changes coagulation balance around perioperative anticoagulation management.
condition_rules.action: Coordinate supplement use with surgeon and stop unless explicitly instructed to continue.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Vitamin K can oppose anticoagulant therapy and alter INR control.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not start/stop without clinician-guided anticoagulation monitoring.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Longer half-life menaquinone forms may have prolonged interaction potential with anticoagulants.
drug_class_rules.action: Escalate anticoagulant interaction warning for MK-7 forms.

condition_rules.mechanism: Yohimbe has stimulant and hemodynamic effects with concerning maternal-fetal risk profile.
condition_rules.action: Do not use during pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Adrenergic stimulation may increase blood pressure and cardiovascular risk.
condition_rules.action: Do not use in hypertension or cardiovascular risk settings.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Yohimbe can counter blood pressure-lowering effects.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid co-use with antihypertensive medications.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Safety data are limited and stimulant effects raise risk concerns.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid in pregnancy and lactation.

condition_rules.mechanism: Concentrated ginger extracts may affect platelet function.
condition_rules.action: Review pre-op supplement use.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Potential additive bleeding tendency.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor with clinician guidance.

condition_rules.mechanism: Caulophyllum thalictroides contains caulosaponin and methylcytisine, which stimulate uterine contractions. Case reports document neonatal stroke, heart failure, and multi-organ injury following maternal use near term.
condition_rules.action: Do not use during pregnancy.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Uterotonic glycosides (caulosaponin) and nicotinic alkaloids (methylcytisine) pose direct fetal risk including cardiac toxicity and CNS injury.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Multiple case reports of serious neonatal adverse events. ACOG and NCCIH advise against use in pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Artemisia vulgaris contains thujone and other volatile terpenoids with emmenagogue and abortifacient activity. Animal studies demonstrate uterotonic effects.
condition_rules.action: Do not use during pregnancy.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Thujone-containing Artemisia species have documented emmenagogue activity. Traditional abortifacient use is well-described historically.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid all Artemisia vulgaris preparations during pregnancy and lactation.

condition_rules.mechanism: Ruta graveolens contains furanoquinoline alkaloids (dictamnine, skimmianine) and furanocoumarins with established abortifacient activity. Animal studies confirm embryotoxic and teratogenic effects at moderate doses.
condition_rules.action: Do not use during pregnancy.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Ruta alkaloids cause uterine smooth muscle contraction and have embryotoxic effects demonstrated in animal models.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Rue is a well-documented traditional abortifacient. Avoid all preparations during pregnancy and lactation.

condition_rules.mechanism: Kavalactones (from Piper methysticum) inhibit CYP1A2, CYP2C9, CYP2C19, CYP2D6, and CYP3A4, disrupting hepatic drug metabolism. Mechanistically, kavalactones activate the pregnane X receptor (PXR) and undergo bioactivation via CYP3A4 to reactive metabolites that form protein adducts in hepatocytes, inducing mitochondrial dysfunction and hepatocellular necrosis. Over 100 spontaneous reports of severe hepatotoxicity have been documented in Western nations, including cases progressing to fulminant hepatic failure requiring liver transplantation. Alcohol co-ingestion potentiates toxicity. Patients with pre-existing liver disease face dramatically amplified risk from both reduced clearance and baseline hepatocellular vulnerability.
condition_rules.action: Contraindicated in patients with any chronic liver disease. Do not use kava supplements alongside alcohol or other hepatotoxic agents. FDA and European regulatory agencies have issued warnings against kava use in liver disease.

condition_rules.mechanism: Kavalactones have demonstrated anticonvulsant properties in animal models via GABA-A receptor modulation. However, abrupt discontinuation after regular use may produce withdrawal seizures. Additionally, unpredictable CNS effects make kava use in epilepsy patients risky without neurologist oversight.
condition_rules.action: Avoid kava in seizure disorders without neurologist supervision. Do not abruptly discontinue if already in use.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Kavalactones enhance ligand binding at GABA-A receptors, block voltage-gated sodium channels, inhibit calcium ion channels, and reduce norepinephrine reuptake — multiple CNS depressant mechanisms overlapping with benzodiazepines and other sedatives. Potent additive CNS depression, ataxia, respiratory depression risk with concurrent sedatives.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not combine kava with sedatives, benzodiazepines, alcohol, or opioids. Risk of potent additive CNS depression. Contraindicated with prescription sedative-hypnotics.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Kava has CNS-active constituents and documented hepatotoxic potential. No safe dose in pregnancy or lactation has been established.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid kava supplements entirely during pregnancy and lactation.

condition_rules.mechanism: Valerian root (Valeriana officinalis) is associated with rare idiosyncratic hepatotoxicity based on published case reports. The mechanism is not fully established; isovaleric acid and valerenic acid undergo hepatic oxidation via CYP2C9, and valerian also contains small amounts of hepatotoxic alkaloids. The overall incidence of liver injury is low, but patients with pre-existing liver disease have reduced hepatic reserve and may be at higher risk from idiosyncratic reactions.
condition_rules.action: Monitor liver function (ALT, AST) if valerian is used long-term in patients with liver disease. Discontinue immediately if transaminase elevations are detected.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Valerenic acid from valerian root increases GABA modulation at GABA-A receptors, sharing a pharmacological mechanism with benzodiazepines. Additive CNS depression, sedation, and psychomotor impairment may occur when combined with sedative drugs.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid combining valerian with prescription sedatives or sleep medications without physician oversight. Do not drive or operate machinery when combining these agents.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Insufficient safety data for use during pregnancy and lactation; valerian's valepotriates have shown cytotoxic potential in vitro.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid valerian during pregnancy and breastfeeding due to insufficient safety data.

condition_rules.mechanism: Saw palmetto (Serenoa repens) has been associated with rare cholestatic hepatitis and, in at least one case, simultaneous acute hepatitis and pancreatitis with rechallenge confirmation. The mechanism is likely idiosyncratic and related to lipophilic fatty acid esters in the extract undergoing hepatic esterase hydrolysis. Standard rat studies at 2–5x human doses showed no liver toxicity markers, suggesting the risk is low but not zero. Pre-existing liver disease may reduce the threshold for idiosyncratic reactions.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in liver disease. Monitor baseline and periodic LFTs (ALT, ALP, bilirubin) if saw palmetto is initiated in patients with liver disease. Discontinue at first sign of cholestasis (jaundice, elevated ALP, pruritus).

condition_rules.mechanism: A published case report documents severe intraoperative hemorrhage in a patient taking saw palmetto (Serenoa repens), with prolonged bleeding time that normalized after discontinuation. The proposed mechanism involves COX pathway inhibition by the lipidosterolic extract, which may reduce thromboxane-mediated platelet activation. However, a randomized in vivo study of standard-dose saw palmetto (manufacturer's recommended dose, 2 weeks) found no measurable effect on PFA-100 platelet function assay in healthy adults.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in patients with bleeding disorders. The evidence is based on a case report; risk may be dose- and context-dependent. Discuss with a healthcare provider before use.

condition_rules.mechanism: A case report of severe intraoperative hemorrhage associated with saw palmetto use prompted awareness of potential perioperative bleeding risk. Bleeding time was prolonged and normalized after discontinuation. One RCT (TURP setting) showed pretreatment with Serenoa repens significantly reduced perioperative blood loss, suggesting complex vascular/anti-inflammatory rather than purely antiplatelet effects.
condition_rules.action: Inform your surgeon of saw palmetto use before planned surgery. Based on the case report, consider discontinuing 1-2 weeks before elective procedures as a precaution, though the evidence level is limited.

condition_rules.mechanism: Saw palmetto (Serenoa repens) inhibits 5-alpha-reductase (both type 1 and type 2), reducing conversion of testosterone to the more potent dihydrotestosterone (DHT). In males, this mechanism is the basis for its use in benign prostatic hyperplasia and androgenic alopecia. However, 5-alpha-reductase inhibition in men who are TTC can impair semen quality, reduce seminal fluid volume, and potentially reduce sperm motility. Additionally, saw palmetto may have anti-androgenic effects that interfere with male reproductive hormone signaling needed for spermatogenesis.
condition_rules.action: Men who are actively trying to conceive should discontinue saw palmetto supplementation. 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors (including saw palmetto) may adversely affect semen parameters and male fertility. Discuss with reproductive urologist if fertility is a concern.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Theoretical concern based on the case report of bleeding and proposed COX-mediated antiplatelet activity. No documented case reports of saw palmetto altering INR or warfarin activity. Risk is considered low at standard doses.
drug_class_rules.action: Inform your prescriber if taking saw palmetto while on anticoagulant therapy. Monitor for unusual bleeding or bruising.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Theoretical additive antiplatelet effect through COX pathway inhibition. The case report of prolonged bleeding time supports caution, though in vivo platelet function studies at standard doses showed no significant effect.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for signs of increased bleeding when combining saw palmetto with antiplatelet medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Theoretical additive COX inhibition. Both saw palmetto lipidosterolic extract and NSAIDs affect prostaglandin synthesis. Clinical significance is uncertain at standard saw palmetto doses.
drug_class_rules.action: No specific dose adjustment required. Inform your healthcare provider if combining these agents.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Saw palmetto has antiandrogenic activity; safety data in pregnancy and lactation are absent.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Not recommended during pregnancy or breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: Dietary turmeric and culinary curcumin use is generally safe and even hepatoprotective at low doses. However, high-dose concentrated curcumin supplements — particularly formulations co-administered with piperine (black pepper extract), which can increase curcumin bioavailability up to 20-fold — have been implicated in drug-induced liver injury (DILI) case reports with cholestatic and hepatocellular patterns. The proposed mechanism involves CYP1A2 and CYP3A4 inhibition by curcumin, leading to reactive metabolite accumulation. Patients with existing liver disease may have impaired detoxification capacity, lowering the safety threshold.
condition_rules.action: Culinary turmeric is acceptable. Avoid high-dose curcumin supplement formulations (>500 mg curcumin/day) especially those combined with piperine in patients with liver disease. Monitor LFTs if high-dose curcumin is initiated.

condition_rules.mechanism: Curcumin, the active principle of turmeric, inhibits platelet aggregation induced by multiple agonists including arachidonic acid (AA), platelet-activating factor (PAF), collagen, ADP, and epinephrine. The primary mechanisms are (1) inhibition of thromboxane A2 (TXA2) synthesis via COX-1 suppression, (2) inhibition of Ca2+ mobilization in platelets, and (3) suppression of 12-lipoxygenase and 5-lipoxygenase. At concentrated supplement doses, antiplatelet effects are clinically relevant.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in patients with bleeding disorders. High-dose curcumin supplements (>500 mg/day curcumin extract) may meaningfully increase bleeding risk. Avoid in patients with active bleeding.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Turmeric/curcumin may potentiate warfarin's anticoagulant effect through additive platelet inhibition (TXA2 suppression, COX-1 inhibition) and possible modulation of hepatic CYP2C9 metabolism of S-warfarin. The combination is listed as a potential warfarin-potentiating herb in systematic reviews covering 149 herb-warfarin interaction reports.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor INR when starting or increasing turmeric supplementation while on warfarin. Inform your prescriber. High-dose curcumin extracts (>500 mg curcumin/day) represent higher risk than culinary turmeric.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Curcumin independently inhibits platelet aggregation via TXA2 suppression, COX-1 inhibition, and Ca2+ signaling interference. Combined with antiplatelet drugs (aspirin, clopidogrel), this produces additive platelet inhibition.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for signs of increased bleeding when combining turmeric supplements with antiplatelet medications. High-dose curcumin extracts carry more risk than dietary turmeric.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Curcumin inhibits both COX-1 and 5-lipoxygenase, pathways shared with NSAIDs. Combined use may produce additive GI mucosal effects and platelet inhibition, modestly increasing GI bleeding risk.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for GI symptoms when combining turmeric/curcumin supplements with NSAIDs. Take NSAIDs with food.

condition_rules.mechanism: Silymarin (the active flavonolignan complex from Silybum marianum) is hepatoprotective and widely used in liver disease patients — it reduces oxidative stress, inhibits inflammatory signaling, and has antifibrotic properties. However, silymarin and its constituents (especially silybin A, isosilybin A/B, silychristin) are mechanism-based inhibitors of CYP3A4 and also inhibit P-glycoprotein. In liver disease patients who are on hepatically-cleared medications (antivirals, immunosuppressants, anticoagulants), this CYP3A4 inhibition can elevate drug plasma levels to toxic ranges. The herb itself does not cause hepatotoxicity at standard doses.
condition_rules.action: Milk thistle is generally beneficial in liver disease but requires monitoring for drug interactions in patients on CYP3A4-metabolized medications. Review the full medication list before initiating. Preferred formulation: standardized silymarin 70–80%, 140–420 mg/day.

condition_rules.mechanism: EPA and DHA competitively inhibit arachidonic acid metabolism, suppressing thromboxane A2 (TXA2) synthesis and reducing platelet aggregation. At doses >3 g/day of combined EPA+DHA, clinically measurable prolongation of bleeding time has been observed. Additionally, omega-3 PUFAs may decrease circulating factor VII levels, further impairing the coagulation cascade.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in patients with bleeding disorders. Doses above 3 g/day of EPA+DHA may significantly increase bleeding risk. Consult a healthcare provider before use.

condition_rules.mechanism: Antiplatelet effects of EPA and DHA via TXA2 suppression may theoretically increase perioperative bleeding risk. Large randomized trials (OPERA, n=1516) found no significant increase in surgical bleeding, but case series with high-dose use (6 g/day) combined with other anticoagulants have reported serious hemorrhagic events including subdural hematoma.
condition_rules.action: Inform your surgeon of fish oil use before planned procedures. High-dose fish oil (>3 g/day) combined with other anticoagulants or antiplatelets warrants discussion with the surgical team. Current evidence does not mandate routine discontinuation for standard doses.

condition_rules.mechanism: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) have established cardioprotective effects, reducing major cardiovascular events (RR 0.95), MI (RR 0.90), and cardiovascular death (RR 0.94) in meta-analysis of 15 RCTs. However, high-dose prescription omega-3 supplementation (≥3g EPA+DHA/day) is associated with a significantly increased risk of atrial fibrillation (RR 1.25, 95% CI 1.10–1.41). This dose-dependent AFib risk is most prominent in populations with existing cardiovascular disease.
condition_rules.action: Low to moderate doses (≤2g/day) are generally cardioprotective. High doses (≥3g/day) should only be used under prescriber supervision with ECG monitoring for AFib in patients with heart disease or existing AFib risk factors.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Omega-3 fatty acids may enhance warfarin's anticoagulant effect through two mechanisms: (1) additive antiplatelet activity via TXA2 pathway suppression, and (2) possible reduction in vitamin K-dependent coagulation factor VII levels. Multiple case reports document INR elevation (from therapeutic range to >4) when fish oil dose was doubled or added to stable warfarin therapy. One case documented INR of 8.06 after initiating fish oil with warfarin.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor INR more frequently if starting, stopping, or changing the dose of fish oil while on warfarin. Inform your prescriber of fish oil supplementation. At doses >2 g/day, consider closer INR surveillance.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive inhibition of platelet aggregation. Both omega-3s and antiplatelet drugs (aspirin, clopidogrel) independently suppress TXA2-mediated platelet activation. A case report of subdural hematoma after a minor fall involved high-dose omega-3 (6 g/day) combined with both aspirin and warfarin.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid high-dose fish oil (>3 g/day) when combined with antiplatelet medications unless under medical supervision. Monitor for signs of unusual bleeding or bruising.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Both omega-3 fatty acids and NSAIDs affect prostaglandin synthesis through COX pathway modulation. The additive reduction in thromboxane production and potential combined effect on GI mucosal prostaglandins may modestly increase GI bleeding risk when used together.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for GI symptoms (stomach pain, dark stools) when combining fish oil with NSAIDs. Take NSAIDs with food.

condition_rules.mechanism: EPA and DHA competitively inhibit arachidonic acid metabolism, suppressing thromboxane A2 (TXA2) synthesis and reducing platelet aggregation. At doses >3 g/day of combined EPA+DHA, clinically measurable prolongation of bleeding time has been observed. Fish oil may also reduce factor VII levels, a vitamin K-dependent coagulation factor.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in patients with bleeding disorders. Doses above 3 g/day of EPA+DHA may significantly increase bleeding risk. Consult a healthcare provider before use.

condition_rules.mechanism: Antiplatelet effects via TXA2 suppression may theoretically increase perioperative bleeding risk. A large RCT (OPERA, n=1516) found no significant increase in surgical bleeding at standard doses; however, high-dose use (6 g/day) combined with anticoagulants has produced serious hemorrhagic events.
condition_rules.action: Inform your surgeon of fish oil use before planned procedures. High-dose fish oil (>3 g/day) combined with other anticoagulants or antiplatelets warrants discussion with the surgical team.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Fish oil (EPA+DHA) may enhance warfarin's anticoagulant effect via additive antiplatelet activity and possible reduction of factor VII. Multiple case reports document INR elevation (to >4 or 8) when fish oil was added or dose-escalated during stable warfarin therapy. One fatal case involved warfarin-induced coagulopathy that could not be reversed after head trauma in a patient also taking fish oil.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor INR more frequently when starting, stopping, or changing fish oil dose during warfarin therapy. Inform prescriber. At doses >2 g/day, closer INR surveillance is warranted.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive inhibition of platelet aggregation through independent TXA2 suppression. Combined use of high-dose fish oil with aspirin and warfarin has been associated with subdural hematoma after minor trauma.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid high-dose fish oil (>3 g/day) when combined with antiplatelet medications unless under medical supervision. Monitor for unusual bleeding or bruising.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Both fish oil and NSAIDs affect prostaglandin synthesis via COX pathway modulation. Combined use may modestly increase GI bleeding risk through additive reduction in mucosal protective prostaglandins.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for GI symptoms when combining fish oil with NSAIDs. Take NSAIDs with food.

condition_rules.mechanism: Curcumin preferentially inhibits platelet-activating factor (PAF)- and arachidonic acid (AA)-induced platelet aggregation with IC50 values of 20-25 µM. Mechanisms include: (1) inhibition of TXA2 synthesis (IC50 ~70 µM for TXA2 formation), (2) blockade of intracellular Ca2+ mobilization via the ionophore pathway, (3) inhibition of 12-lipoxygenase and 5-lipoxygenase, and (4) reduction of serotonin levels in platelets. Curcuminoids (the standardized extract) are approximately 2-fold more potent than curcumin alone in inhibiting platelet aggregation.
condition_rules.action: Avoid curcumin extracts in active bleeding disorders. Use with caution in patients with underlying coagulopathy or thrombocytopenia. Dietary turmeric at culinary doses is generally not a concern.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Curcumin may enhance warfarin effect through additive antiplatelet activity (TXA2 suppression, COX-1 inhibition, 12-LOX inhibition) and potential CYP2C9 modulation affecting S-warfarin metabolism. The concentrated extract form poses higher interaction risk than dietary turmeric. Warfarin potentiation by turmeric/curcumin is documented in herb-drug interaction systematic reviews.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor INR when initiating or escalating curcumin supplementation during warfarin therapy. Inform prescriber. Consider temporary INR check 1-2 weeks after starting curcumin extract.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Curcumin independently inhibits platelet aggregation via TXA2 suppression, COX-1, 5-LOX, and 12-LOX inhibition, and Ca2+ signaling blockade. Additive inhibition with antiplatelet drugs (aspirin, clopidogrel, ticagrelor) is mechanistically predicted and supported by in vitro data.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for signs of increased bleeding when combining curcumin supplements with antiplatelet medications. Avoid high-dose curcumin extracts (>1 g curcumin/day) without medical supervision in patients on dual antiplatelet therapy.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Curcumin inhibits COX-1 and 5-LOX, pathways also targeted by NSAIDs. Concurrent use may produce additive GI mucosal effects and platelet suppression, with modest increased risk of GI bleeding.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for GI symptoms (stomach pain, dark stools) when combining curcumin supplements with NSAIDs.

condition_rules.mechanism: Boswellic acids (BAs), particularly acetyl-11-keto-beta-boswellic acid (AKBA), inhibit COX-1 product formation in intact human platelets (IC50 ~6 µM) through direct reversible binding to the COX-1 enzyme. BAs are also potent specific inhibitors of 5-lipoxygenase (5-LO; IC50 1.5 µM for AKBA), reducing leukotriene B4 synthesis. This dual COX-1/5-LOX inhibition profile suggests meaningful antiplatelet potential at supplemental doses. Unlike NSAIDs, BAs do not inhibit the peroxidation of arachidonic acid, making them non-redox inhibitors.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in patients with bleeding disorders. High-dose Boswellia extract standardized for AKBA may inhibit platelet function via COX-1. Consult a healthcare provider before use.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Theoretical concern based on COX-1 inhibition in platelets and 5-LOX inhibition that could additively suppress hemostatic mechanisms when combined with anticoagulants. No clinical case reports of warfarin-boswellia interaction have been identified. The AKBA-COX-1 interaction is mechanistically relevant but clinical INR impact is unconfirmed.
drug_class_rules.action: Inform your prescriber if using Boswellia supplements while on anticoagulant therapy. Monitor for unusual bruising or bleeding.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: AKBA directly and reversibly inhibits COX-1 in human platelets, the same enzyme targeted by aspirin (irreversibly) and NSAIDs. Combined use with antiplatelet agents produces mechanistically additive platelet inhibition through COX-1 suppression.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for signs of increased bleeding when combining Boswellia supplements with antiplatelet medications. Exercise caution with high-AKBA standardized extracts.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Boswellic acids inhibit COX-1 (AKBA) and 5-LOX, pathways also targeted by NSAIDs. Combined use produces additive arachidonic acid pathway suppression and may modestly increase GI bleeding risk through complementary mechanisms.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for GI symptoms when combining Boswellia with NSAIDs. The combination is mechanistically rational for anti-inflammatory effect but carries additive GI mucosal risk.

condition_rules.mechanism: Evening primrose oil (EPO) contains gamma-linolenic acid (GLA), a precursor to dihomo-gamma-linolenic acid (DGLA) and prostaglandin E1 (PGE1). GLA shifts eicosanoid metabolism away from arachidonic acid-derived TXA2, with PGE1 inhibiting platelet aggregation. Animal studies show EPO significantly extends prothrombin time, activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT), and thrombin time, and decreases platelet count. EPO also demonstrated significant antiplatelet activity in a celecoxib-cardiovascular risk model.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in patients with bleeding disorders or coagulopathy. High-dose EPO (>3 g/day) may affect coagulation parameters. Consult a healthcare provider before use.

condition_rules.mechanism: Evening primrose oil (EPO) contains gamma-linolenic acid (GLA). Concern regarding seizure threshold lowering originated from early case reports in the 1980s. A 2007 review concluded the association was likely spurious and that GLA and linoleic acid may actually have anticonvulsant properties. Risk is low but seizure disorder patients warrant monitoring and physician discussion.
condition_rules.action: Evening primrose oil is not absolutely contraindicated in seizure disorders based on current evidence, but patients with epilepsy should discuss use with their neurologist. Monitor for any changes in seizure control.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: EPO's GLA-derived PGE1 inhibits platelet aggregation and EPO has demonstrated anti-coagulant effects on multiple coagulation parameters (PT, aPTT, thrombin time) in animal studies. Combined with warfarin, additive anticoagulation could result. Listed among herbs with potential warfarin potentiation in clinical pharmacology references.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor coagulation status if taking EPO with warfarin or other anticoagulants. Inform prescriber of EPO supplementation.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: GLA-derived eicosanoids (PGE1) inhibit platelet aggregation. Combined with antiplatelet drugs, additive platelet inhibition may occur. The clinical magnitude at standard EPO doses (1-3 g/day) is uncertain but mechanistically plausible.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for signs of increased bleeding when combining EPO with antiplatelet medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Both EPO (via PGE1 and arachidonic acid competition) and NSAIDs affect prostaglandin pathways. In rat models, EPO was shown to protect against celecoxib-induced cardiovascular thrombogenic risk, suggesting complementary prostaglandin modulation. Combined use may produce complex prostaglandin effects.
drug_class_rules.action: No specific dose adjustment required. Inform your healthcare provider if combining EPO with NSAIDs, especially at high doses.

condition_rules.mechanism: Vitamin D does not directly affect platelet aggregation at physiological or supplemental doses. However, high-dose vitamin D supplementation may interact with warfarin sensitivity in patients with concurrent vitamin K deficiency by affecting the vitamin K-dependent carboxylation of coagulation factors (II, VII, IX, X). The vitamin D endocrine system and vitamin K share overlapping roles in gamma-carboxylation of hepatic Gla-proteins.
condition_rules.action: Vitamin D at standard supplemental doses (up to 4000 IU/day) is not a clinically significant bleeding risk on its own. Monitor if taking high-dose vitamin D (>10,000 IU/day) with anticoagulants.

condition_rules.mechanism: Vitamin D exerts broad immunomodulatory effects, generally shifting immune responses toward tolerance via Treg cell upregulation and Th17 suppression. Low vitamin D status is associated with increased autoimmune risk. Supplementation may be beneficial in Hashimoto thyroiditis and multiple sclerosis, but high doses in active autoimmune disease require monitoring.
condition_rules.action: Vitamin D supplementation at standard doses (1000–4000 IU/day) is generally safe in autoimmune conditions. High-dose vitamin D requires physician-monitored 25-OH vitamin D levels to avoid toxicity.

condition_rules.mechanism: Vitamin D deficiency is associated with increased cardiovascular risk, and moderate supplementation is generally considered beneficial. However, pharmacologic doses (>4000 IU/day chronically) can cause hypercalcemia, which promotes vascular calcification and may worsen coronary artery disease. The benefit-risk ratio is dose-dependent.
condition_rules.action: Target serum 25(OH)D levels of 30–60 ng/mL. Avoid high-dose supplementation (>4000 IU/day) without clinical monitoring of calcium and 25(OH)D levels.

condition_rules.mechanism: Vitamin D receptors (VDR) are expressed in ovarian granulosa cells, endometrium, and placenta. A 2017 review (Voulgaris et al., Hormones) summarized strong associations between vitamin D status and PCOS metabolic parameters, endometriosis, and IVF outcomes, with deficiency linked to lower clinical pregnancy rates. Meta-analyses of IVF studies suggest women with 25(OH)D >20 ng/mL have significantly better outcomes, though causality is not fully established.
condition_rules.action: Check 25(OH)D levels prior to or during TTC. Correct deficiency (<20 ng/mL) with supplementation (1000–2000 IU/day) under clinician guidance. Achieving sufficiency (30–60 ng/mL) is recommended before and during early pregnancy. Standard prenatal vitamins provide ~400–1000 IU/day.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: High-dose vitamin D (>10,000 IU/day) has been associated with altered warfarin sensitivity in some patients. The proposed mechanism involves shared metabolic pathways with vitamin K: vitamin D may modulate hepatic expression of vitamin K-dependent coagulation factors (II, VII, IX, X), and vitamin D deficiency itself affects coagulation factor synthesis. Vitamin D and K have documented synergistic effects on Gla-protein carboxylation, and disruption of this balance at supraphysiological vitamin D doses may affect INR stability in warfarin patients.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor INR stability when significantly changing vitamin D supplementation dose, particularly at high doses (>10,000 IU/day). Routine supplementation doses (1000-4000 IU/day) typically do not require INR monitoring changes.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: No established direct interaction between vitamin D supplementation and antiplatelet drug activity. Monitoring recommended at high doses due to broader hemostatic pathway involvement.
drug_class_rules.action: No specific action required at standard supplemental doses. Monitor for unusual bleeding if taking high-dose vitamin D with antiplatelet medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: No established pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic interaction between vitamin D and NSAIDs on platelet or coagulation pathways. Theoretical concern at high doses given shared anti-inflammatory regulatory roles.
drug_class_rules.action: No specific action required at standard supplemental doses.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Vitamin D has additive immunomodulatory effects that may complement immunosuppressant therapy. Standard-dose supplementation is generally considered safe alongside immunosuppressants and may reduce corticosteroid-induced bone loss.
drug_class_rules.action: Standard vitamin D supplementation is appropriate alongside immunosuppressants. Monitor 25-OH vitamin D levels annually. High-dose supplementation (>4000 IU/day) requires physician oversight.

condition_rules.mechanism: Glucosamine does not directly inhibit platelet aggregation or the coagulation cascade via known pharmacological mechanisms. The bleeding risk in patients with bleeding disorders is primarily indirect — through potentiation of anticoagulant medications (warfarin), as documented in multiple case reports and FDA/WHO pharmacovigilance databases.
condition_rules.action: Glucosamine at standard doses (1500 mg/day) is not a direct bleeding risk for most patients with bleeding disorders. Use caution if also taking anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Multiple case reports and pharmacovigilance data document glucosamine-associated INR elevation in patients on stable warfarin therapy. A systematic pharmacovigilance review (Knudsen & Sokol, 2008) identified 20 FDA MedWatch reports and 21 WHO reports of altered coagulation (increased INR, bleeding) with glucosamine and warfarin, including one case of intraventricular bleed and subdural hematoma. A dose-response relationship was observed: one patient's INR rose from 2.3 to 3.9 after dose escalation from 1000 to 3000 mg/day, then to 4.7 on further dose reduction delay, normalizing after glucosamine discontinuation. The mechanism is unknown — glucosamine is not a known inhibitor of CYP2C9 or CYP3A4.
drug_class_rules.action: Inform your prescriber if taking glucosamine while on warfarin. Check INR within 2-4 weeks of starting or stopping glucosamine. Dose escalation of glucosamine may destabilize previously stable anticoagulation control.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: No documented direct interaction between glucosamine and antiplatelet drugs. Theoretical concern given unknown mechanism of warfarin potentiation.
drug_class_rules.action: No specific dose adjustment required. Inform prescriber of glucosamine use if on antiplatelet therapy.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Glucosamine is frequently co-administered with chondroitin for osteoarthritis, often as an alternative to NSAIDs. No known direct pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic interaction with NSAIDs on coagulation.
drug_class_rules.action: No specific action required. Glucosamine may allow dose reduction of NSAIDs in osteoarthritis management.

condition_rules.mechanism: Chamomile (Matricaria chamomilla) contains natural coumarins including herniarin and umbelliferone, which share structural similarity with warfarin. Additionally, chamomile contains flavonoids (apigenin, quercetin) that may inhibit platelet aggregation. A systematic review of herb-warfarin interactions identified chamomile tea as one of the herbs associated with bleeding events and INR elevation. A case of multiple thromboses and hemorrhage in a patient consuming chamomile tea (also taking warfarin) was reported.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in patients with bleeding disorders. Concentrated chamomile extracts and large volumes of chamomile tea represent more risk than occasional use. Avoid in active bleeding.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Chamomile contains natural coumarins (herniarin, umbelliferone) that may have additive anticoagulant effect with warfarin. A systematic review of 149 herb-warfarin interaction reports specifically identifies chamomile tea as causing warfarin potentiation with bleeding events. The 2000 AJHP review (Heck et al.) lists chamomile among herbs that may potentially increase bleeding or potentiate warfarin effects.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid large amounts of chamomile tea (>4 cups/day) or concentrated chamomile extracts while on warfarin. Inform your prescriber. Monitor INR if chamomile consumption is substantial or newly started.
